coredata.dc:title: Chemically modified chitosan‑sodium alginate as chemo-sensor adsorbent for the detection of picric acid and removal of biebrich scarlet
coredata.dc:creator: Priya,
Sharma, Amit Kumar
Kaith, Balbir Singh
Vipula,
Chandel, Kajal
Singh, Anoop
Isha,
coredata.dc:description: We report here on the green synthesis method of hydrogel nanocomposite (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQDs) incorporated with carbon quantum dots for picric acid sensing and biebrich scharlet dye removal from industrial waste water. The nanocomposite precursor was derived from chitosan‑sodium alginate hybrid backbone and acrylamide monomer through the formation of semi-IPN matrix (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm). The swelling percentage of semi-IPN can be controlled through process parameters such as reaction time, temperature, pH, water volume, acrylamide, N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide and ammonium persulphate concentrations. Hydrogel nanocomposite displayed 99% quenching efficiency for the selective detection of picric acid and 96% dye removal efficiency was found to be observed for the removal of biebrich scarlet dye. The sorption was found to be favorable though Freundlich and Temkin adsorption isotherms along with the 2nd order kinetics. The devices were found to be recyclable and reusable up to six consecutive cycles indicating their applicability in textile industries for the treatment of effluents.

head.ce:title: Chemically modified chitosan‑sodium alginate as chemo-sensor adsorbent for the detection of picric acid and removal of biebrich scarlet

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Dr B R Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar 144 011, Punjab, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr B R Ambedkar National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Dr B R Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar – 144 011 (Punjab), India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report here on the green synthesis method of hydrogel nanocomposite (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQDs) incorporated with carbon quantum dots for picric acid sensing and biebrich scharlet dye removal from industrial waste water. The nanocomposite precursor was derived from chitosan‑sodium alginate hybrid backbone and acrylamide monomer through the formation of semi-IPN matrix (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm). The swelling percentage of semi-IPN can be controlled through process parameters such as reaction time, temperature, pH, water volume, acrylamide, N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide and ammonium persulphate concentrations. Hydrogel nanocomposite displayed 99% quenching efficiency for the selective detection of picric acid and 96% dye removal efficiency was found to be observed for the removal of biebrich scarlet dye. The sorption was found to be favorable though Freundlich and Temkin adsorption isotherms along with the 2nd order kinetics. The devices were found to be recyclable and reusable up to six consecutive cycles indicating their applicability in textile industries for the treatment of effluents.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the enhancement of terrorist activities has created a worldwide threat to the social, environmental and economic aspects of every country. In India, 176% increase in terrorist activities was found to be held in J&K during 2014–2018 and about 40 militants were killed in Pulwama Terrorist Incident. Picric acid is a well-known explosive and is widely used in military operations, forensic investigations pharmaceutics, pesticides and production of dyes [1]. The accumulation of picric acid in natural water during their testing, usage, storage, and dumping can show long-term harmful impacts on the living and non-living species. The presence of nitro and phenol functionalities can cause strong irritation to eyes and skin along with the adverse impact on the respiratory system [2]. Therefore, there is a strong need to develop some sensing materials for the detection of picric acid in order to avoid terrorism activities and to save aquatic life.

Recently, many sensors have been reported for the detection of nitroaromatic explosives including nanosensors [3], electrochemical-based sensors [4], polymer-based sensors [5], fluorescence quenching based sensors [6], nanomaterial surface energy transfer [7] and quantum dot-antibody fluorescence resonance energy transfer [8]. Out of these, fluorescence quenching based sensors are of great importance due to their low cost, high selectivity and high sensitivity. Hydrogel based fluorescent sensors can find applications for the detection of toxic heavy metal ions such as Pb2+, Cu2+, Ni2+ and Hg2+ [9–11]. They are equally important for the detection of glucose and pH level in the body [12,13].

The spillage of toxic textile dyes from the textile industries to our natural water resources can produce adverse impacts on our environment. Most of the dyes are highly water soluble and can create a harmful impact on living and non-living species. Due to very complex structure, the biodegradation of such pollutants is not an easy task and therefore the harmful impacts can be seen on the aquatic species for a longer duration of time [14]. The biebrich scarlet dye is used to color nonpolar substances such as fats, oils, greases, acrylic emulsions and various hydrocarbon products. It produces carcinogenic impact and called illegal dye for food additive applications. It can also show negative impact on the water bodies if discharges accidentally. Therefore, these pollutants must be removed from the polluted water in order to save the aquatic life. The dye removal can be achieved through adsorption [15], membrane filtration [16], precipitation [17], ozonation, fungal detachment [18] and electrochemical separation [19]. Due to the high pH sensitivity and reversibility of separation process, the smart hydrogel adsorbents can be the best choice for dye removal applications. Natural backbone based hydrogel nanocomposites possess excellent dye removal properties due to the availability of a sufficient number of active sites onto the adsorbent which can effectively hold the dye molecules due to electrostatic interaction between the adsorbent-adsorbate molecules [14].

Hydrogels are three-dimensional crosslinked polymers that do not dissolve but can swell in water and can respond to fluctuations of external stimuli [20–22]. Under corporeal conditions, they are able to retain a large amount of water or biological fluids which can be characterized by a soft rubbery consistency similar to natural tissues, making them a perfect substance for a variety of applications. Natural hydrogels are preferred over the synthetic ones due to their biodegradable nature [23]. Sodium alginate is an anionic copolymer made up of poly-α-1,4-l-guluronic acid and poly-β-1,4-d-mannuronic acid linked through 1–4 glycosidic linkage. It is water soluble, non-toxic, biodegradable, biocompatible and highly abundant natural macromolecule extracted from marine algae/bacteria. Sodium alginate is widely used in biomedical applications such as scaffolds for cell culture, wound dressings and dental impression materials. The structural modification of –OH and –COOH functional groups can easily be done through chemical or physical crosslinking methods including polymer blending, graft copolymerization and compounding with different functional components [24]. Chitosan is a polycationic semi-crystalline polysaccharide comprising (1 → 4)-2-acetamido-2-deoxy-β-D-glucan and (1 → 4)-2-amino-2-deoxy-β-D-glucan units. It is derived from the partial deacetylation of chitin: a natural polymer originated from green algae cell wall, yeasts and some mushroom envelopes. It has been used in a variety of biomedical applications due to its greater biodegradability, biocompatibility and ability to form a gel, membrane, cotton, fiber and microsphere [25].

In the present paper, we have reported the green synthesis of semi-IPN (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm) and carbon quantum dots nanocomposite (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQDs) derived from natural backbones (chitosan and sodium alginate). The fluorescent nanocomposite was analyzed for detecting picric acid through fluorescence quenching experiments. The synthesized samples were also examined for the effective removal of biebrich scarlet dye and the adsorption phenomenon was determined mathematically through the evaluation of kinetic and thermodynamic parameters.

Chitosan (Chit), sodium alginate (Na-Ala) and ethylenediamine (Ed) were acquired from Sigma Aldrich. Acetic acid (Ac), acrylamide (AA), N,N′-methylene bisacrylamide (MBA), ammonium persulphate (APS), picric acid and biebrich scarlet (Bs) were obtained from SD fine chemicals Ltd.

The investigation of the functional group entities was carried out using Carry 630 FTIR Spectrophotometer (range = 400 to 4000 cm−1). The identification of carbon dots inside the polymeric framework was analyzed with Tecnai G2 20 (FEI) S-Twin Transmission Electron Microscope. The qualitative elemental constitution and surface morphology of the polymeric samples were inspected through Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM, FE-SEM QUANTA 200 model). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out utilizing PAN ANALYTICAL X-beam diffractometer. Fluorescence spectral studies were done using Carry 632 Fluorescent spectrophotometer. Carry 100 UV– Vis Spectrophotometer with a quartz cuvette of path length 1 cm−1 (Agilent Technologies) was used to record absorbance spectra of different dye solutions. All the samples were weighed over CPA225D Sartius Analytical Valance.

The semi-IPN (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm) was incorporated in the wake of upgrading the procedure factors through swelling strategy to get the results with maximum swelling percentage. 1 g chitosan‑sodium alginate (1:1, w/w) was blended with double distilled water and acetic acid (9:1, v/v) in 50 mL beaker. The reaction mixture was mixed using mechanical stirrer and blended altogether for around 10 min to get homogeneous slurry. The Inclusion of acrylamide was done gradually to the reaction mixture and this was trailed by the delicate expansion of N, N′-methylene bisacrylamide (MBA). After homogenous mixing, ammonium persulphate was added delicately and the mixture was kept in a hot air oven whose operating temperature was set at 70 ± 2 °C. After 3 h, the semi-IPN (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm) was taken out, washed completely with water to separate out the homopolymer and unreacted waste product. Finally, the synthesized semi-IPN (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm) was dried in hot air oven at 65 ± 2 °C until the sample attains a constant weight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-situ synthesis of carbon dots nano-composite (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of carbon dots of chitosan was carried out using hydrothermal method. 3 g Chitosan-ethylene diamine (1:2, w/w ratio, respectively) was mixed in 50 mL double distilled water. The pH of solution was maintained at 10–11 using 0.1 N NaOH solution and the reaction mixture was autoclaved for 12 h at 180 °C. The obtained pale yellow solution was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 15 min and the synthesized fluorescent carbon dots were separated out from the reaction mixture through filtration. 23 mL filtrate containing fluorescent nanoparticles was taken and the reaction was carried out under pre-optimized reaction conditions for the synthesis of carbon dots nano-composite (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQD). The excess carbon dots located over the surface of semi-IPN matrix were removed through solvent washing followed by drying of nano-composite at 65 ± 2 °C.

The water uptake capacity of semi-IPN (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm) was studied in double distilled water at room temperature and neutral pH. 1.0 g sample was immersed in 100 mL double distilled water and the swelling response of the sample was studied after 6 h time interval using Eq. (1) [26].

In order to achieve maximum dye removal efficacy, the adsorption studies of the dye biebrich scarlet (BS) were accomplished through the optimization of different process parameters i.e. adsorbent dose, contact time, initial dye concentration and pH of the solution. The analysis was performed three times for each process variable at ambient room temperature. After the adsorption of biebrich scarlet through the adsorbent molecules, the remaining dye solution was investigated with Carry 631 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer at 504 nm wavelength for calculating residual dye concentration. The percentage removal of the dye biebrich scarlet was calculated by Eq. (2) [27].

where, Ci is initial dye concentration (mgL−1), Ce is equilibrium dye concentration (mgL−1) of the unabsorbed dye and % RD is dye removal percentage.

The optimization of the adsorbent dose of Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm was carried out by varying its amount from 50 mg to 250 mg in 15 mL of 60 mgL−1 biebrich scarlet dye solution. The maximum dye removal percentage was also accessed with respect to initial dye concentration by varying the concentration from 20 mgL−1 to 100 mgL−1. pH dependent dye removal studies were also carried out in order to see the impact of pH on dye removal percentage. The maximum adsorption capacity (X
e ) of the adsorbent can be derived from Eq. (3).

where Xe (mg g−1) is the adsorption capacity, W (g) is the weight of the adsorbent and V (L) is the volume of dye solution [27].

Thermal decomposition of ammonium persulphate and water resulted in the formation of OH* and SO4*−. The generated free radicals further propagate the chain reaction and produce a large number of active radical centers onto the hybrid backbone, acrylamide and N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide moieties. The graft copolymeric chains of hybrid backbone Na-Ala/Chit got crosslinked with N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide and acrylamide molecules. The crosslinked network structure provided more dimensional stability for the stabilization of carbon quantum dots (Fig. 1 ) [28].

Maximum swelling percentage (Ps = 600%) was achieved after optimizing all the significant process parameters. The optimal values of different reaction parameters are reaction temperature = 70 °C, reaction time = 210 min, pH = 7.0, solvent amount = 19 mL, acrylamide = 1.03663 molL−1, N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide =4.099 × 10−2 molL−1 and ammonium persulphate = 4.77 × 10−2 molL−1.

The optimum reaction temperature and time were found to be 70 °C and 210 min with the highest swelling percentage (Ps) of 427% and 474%, respectively. The optimization for the reaction temperature was carried out by varying the temperature from 60 °C to 80 °C. Other variables were kept constant i.e. acrylamide concentration = 1.0049 molL−1, solvent = 21 mL, time = 150 min, pH = 7.0, N,N′-methylene bisacrylamide concentration = 3.0880 × 10−2 molL−1 and ammonium persulphate concentration = 7.2074× 10−2 molL−1 (Fig. 2a). Likewise, the optimization of reaction time was carried out by varying the time from 150 min to 270 min and keeping the other parameters constant. The constant values of reaction parameters were: acrylamide concentration = 1.0049 molL−1, solvent = 21 mL, temperature = 70 °C, pH = 7.0, N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide concentration = 3.0880 × 10−2 and ammonium persulphate concentration = 7.2074 × 10−2 molL−1 (Fig. 2b). The percentage swelling was initially found to increase with the increase in reaction temperature and time up to the optimal values and then this was followed by a sudden decrease in the percentage swelling behavior. The initial increment in the swelling percentage was due to the generation of more number of hydroxyl free radicals and sulphate ion free radicals which have sufficient time to interact with the hybrid backbone and monomeric moieties resulting in the formation of a large number of crosslinks. But, beyond the optimum values, increased temperature and time resulted in decreased percentage swelling due to the formation of highly compact cross-linked structures [29,30].

The optimum solvent for the reaction medium was found to be 19 mL with highest percentage swelling (500%). Solvent volume was optimized through the variation of water content from 15 mL to 23 mL and keeping the other parameters constant (acrylamide concentration = 1.004 molL−1, reaction temperature = 70 °C, reaction time = 210 min, pH = 7.0, N,N′-methylene bisacrylamide concentration = 3.4169 × 10−2 and ammonium persulphate concentration = 7.9665 × 10−2 molL−1). Fig. 2c clearly indicates that the percentage swelling increases initially with the increase in water volume, but, after attaining the maxima, a decrease in percentage swelling was observed. This type of behavior was due to the production of more number of hydroxyl free radicals (*OH) propagating the chain reaction for the formation of 3-D crosslinked network. The decreased swelling percentage after achieving the maxima was due to the excessive generation of *OH free radicals that favors chain termination reaction instead of chain propagation reaction [31].

In order to see the impact of pH on the swelling percentage of the hydrogel, the pH of the reaction medium was varied from 3.0 to 11.0. The constant values of other reaction parameters were acrylamide concentration = 1.0366 molL−1, reaction temperature = 65 °C, reaction time = 210 min, Solvent = 19 mL, N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide concentration = 3.4139 × 10−2 molL−1 and ammonium persulphate = 4.77× 10−2 molL−1. The optimum pH of the reaction medium was found to be 7.0. with highest percentage swelling (Ps = 520%) and a decrease in swelling percentage was observed with either increase or decrease in pH of the reaction medium (Fig. 2d).

The osmotic pressure theory well describes the osmotic pressure of a weakly charged hydrogel Eq. (4).
(4)
π ion
= RTΣ
C ig
−
C is
where, the symbol πion, R, T, Cig and Cis represents osmotic swelling pressure, gas constant, absolute temperature, the concentration of mobile ions in swollen state and concentration of mobile ions in the external solution, respectively.

Acidic functional moieties i.e. –COO– ions are responsible for overall swelling and de-swelling properties of the superabsorbents. In a neutral medium, most of the charged –COO– ions tend to repel one another resulting in to increased pore size for holding a maximum number of water molecules and thus the highest swelling percentage was observed. In acidic medium, structural compactness was observed due to the protonation of carboxylate anions leading to decreased swelling percentage. On other hands, the increase in pH beyond 7.0 resulted in the formation of carboxylate anions in large amount and the predominance of charge screening effect of Na+ ions suppresses the anion-anion repulsions among various carboxylate anions which will otherwise be responsible for increased swelling percentage [32,33].

The optimum concentration of acrylamide, N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide and ammonium persulphate were found to be 1.03663 molL−1, 4.0966× 10−2 mol L−1 and 4.77 × 10−2 molL−1 with highest percentage swelling (Ps) 541%, 600% and 541%, respectively (Fig. 2e). The optimization for the monomer concentration was done by varying its concentration from 0.5923 molL−1 to 1.332 molL−1 and keeping the other parameters constant (reaction temperature = 70 °C, reaction time = 210 min, Solvent = 19 mL, pH = 7.0, N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide concentration = 3.4169 × 10−2 molL−1, ammonium persulphate concentration = 7.9665 × 10−2 molL−1). The amount of N,N′-methylene bisacrylamide was optimized under varying concentrations ranging between 2.731 × 10−2 molL−1 to 6.145 × 10−2 molL−1 and keeping the concentration of other parameters constant (acrylamide concentration = 1.0366 molL−1, reaction temperature = 70 °C, reaction time = 210 min, Solvent = 19 mL, pH = 7.0, ammonium persulphate = 7.9665 × 10−2 molL−1) (Fig. 2g). Similarly, the optimized amount of ammonium persulphate was obtained after varying its concentration from 3.186 × 10−2 molL−1 to 9.5592 × 10−2 molL−1. During the optimization of ammonium persulphate concentration, the constant values of other reaction parameters were acrylamide = 1.03663 molL−1, reaction temperature = 70 °C, reaction time = 210 min, Solvent = 19 mL, pH = 7.0, N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide = 4.0966 × 10−2 molL−1 (Fig. 2g).

In all three cases, fluid uptake capacity increases initially due to the formation of a large number of active free radical species and the approachability of monomer-crosslinker molecules in the vicinity of hybrid backbone Na-Ala/Chit favoring graft copolymerization reaction. But, beyond the optimum values of acrylamide, N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide and ammonium persulphate, a decrease in swelling percentage was observed. Such type of behavior was due to the production of an excessive number of free radical species promoting homopolymerization reactions instead of a crosslinked product. The increased concentration of acrylamide, N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide and ammonium persulphate increases reaction medium viscosity and creates a barrier for the movement of free radicals and activated polymeric chains which resulted in decreased extent of crosslinking and hence the swelling percentage gets reduced [34,35].

Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopic studies (EDS) were performed in order to analyze the elemental compositions of the samples. All the samples were found to possess carbon, oxygen and nitrogen as major constituents. The elemental composition {weight% (atomic%)} of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen in case of hybrid backbone was found to be 46.76% (56.70%), 40.74% (35.70%) and 9.05% (9.08%), respectively (Fig. 3a). After crosslinking with acrylamide, the elemental composition of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen in case of semi-IPN (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm) becomes 49.80% (56.26%), 33.96% (28.81%) and 14.46% (14.01%), respectively (Fig. 3b). The incursion of carbon dots inside the matrix of semi-IPN has also shown its impact and the elemental composition of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen becomes 45.27% (53.81%), 35.36% (31.55%) and 7.06% (7.20%) (Fig. 3c).

The SEM images of hybrid backbone, semi-IPN (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm) and nanocomposite (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQD) are presented in Fig. 3d–f. It is evident from Fig. 3d that the smooth and porous surface of the hybrid backbone becomes rough after crosslinking. The surface roughness clearly indicated the grafting of acrylamide onto the hybrid backbone (Fig. 3e). The non-uniformity and surface roughness increases in the case of nanocomposite (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQD) (Fig. 3f) [36].

Transmission electron microscopic analysis (TEM) was done for the calculation of particle size of carbon quantum dots. The TEM image of the nanocomposite (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQD) is presented in Fig. 3g. A cluster of spherically shaped particles were found to be distributed throughout the matrix of semi-IPN (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm). The average particle size of carbon quantum dots in the present study was found to be 2.52 ± 0.03 nm (Fig. 3g). 3-D crosslinked matrix of semi-IPN provides sufficient space for the stabilization of carbon quantum dots [37]. Fig. 3h shows a histogram representing particle size distribution.

EDS elemental mapping results of the nanocomposite (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQD) have shown the presence of five elements C, O, N, Na and S distributed quite uniformly. The complete elemental distribution of these elements is mentioned in Fig. 3i.

The analysis of different functional groups in chitosan, sodium alginate, hybrid backbone, semi-IPN (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm) and hydrogel-based nanocomposite (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQD) was done with Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic technique (FTIR) and the results are depicted in Fig. 4a. The FTIR spectrum of chitosan depicts the presence of different characteristic peaks at 3363 cm−1 (-N-H stretching of primary amides), 3289 cm−1 (-O-H stretching), 2875 cm−1 (-C-H asymmetric stretching), 1648 cm−1 (-C=O stretch of amide I), 1588 cm−1 (-N-H bending of amide II),1421 cm−1 (-CH2 bending), 1378 cm−1 (-CH2 symmetrical deformations), 1325 cm−1 (-C-N stretching of amide III) and 1027 cm−1 (CO stretching peaks). In case of sodium alginate, the characteristics peaks were found to appear at 3226 cm−1 (–O-H stretching), 2934 cm−1 (-C-H stretching peaks), 1595 cm−1 (asymmetric stretching for carboxylate group), 1407 cm−1 (symmetric stretching for carboxylate group), 1026 cm−1 (CO stretching peak). The FTIR spectrum of hybrid backbone contains all the characteristic peaks of chitosan and sodium alginate. Compared to the hybrid backbone, the FTIR spectrum of crosslinked semi-IPN (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm) have shown major spectral peaks at 3180 cm−1 (-N-H stretching of primary amines), 1603 cm−1 (–C=O stretching peak), 1409 (symmetric stretching for carboxylate group) and 1029 cm−1 (-C-H stretching peak). The slight shifting of the peaks was due to the crosslinking of acrylamide onto the hybrid backbone. Similarly, the shift in peaks was observed for the carbon quantum dots nanocomposite (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQD) [38].

Fig. 4b indicates the XRD patterns of chitosan, sodium alginate, hybrid backbone, semi-IPN (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm) and the nanocomposite (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQD). The % crystallinity (Xc) of the samples was calculated using standard Eq. (5) [39].
(5)

where Ac is the area of crystalline region and Aa is the area of amorphous region. The percentage crystallinity of chitosan, sodium alginate and hybrid backbone was found to be 49.73%, 56.37% and 60.97%, respectively indicating their semi-crystalline nature. The higher percentage crystallinity of semi-IPN (65.94%) and the nanocomposite (67.54%) depicted a more homogeneous and crosslinked structure. A cluster of crystalline peaks that appeared in the XRD patterns of nanocomposite indicated the presence of carbon quantum dots inside the semi-IPN matrix [40].

Fluorescence quenching analysis method was studied in the present case for the selective detection of picric acid using Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQDs nanocomposite. The sensitivity of the nanocomposite was also checked for various organic pollutants such as folic acid, citric acid, ascorbic acid and tartaric acid of 100 μM concentrations. But the maximum fluorescence quenching was observed in case of picric acid indicating the selectivity of the nanocomposite for the detection of picric acid (Fig. 5a). The quenching efficiency of the nanocomposite was calculated using the following Eq. (6).
(6)

where I
o and I represents fluorescence intensities before and after adding analyte.

Fig. 5b represents the effect of analyte concentration on the fluorescence intensity and Fig. 5c shows quenching efficiency with respect to the analyte concentration. 98.89% quenching efficiency was found with 80 μM concentration of picric acid with 150 mg nanocomposite (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQDs) dose.

Forster resonance energy transfer mechanism (FRET) operated for the quenching of Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQDs with the addition of picric acid. In the mechanistic process, the CQDs are in excited state and quencher (picric acid) is in the ground state. The absorption spectrum of quencher overlaps with the emission spectrum of CQDs and the energy transfer takes place in non-radiative manner through dipole-dipole interactions. This mechanism is distance dependent mechanism and applicable when the distance between the excited donor state of fluorophore and acceptor state of quencher lies between 10 and 100 °A. With the increase in concentration of quencher, the extent of dipole-dipole interactions increases. The increased interaction resulted in to most probable overlapping of absorption-emission bands of quencher-fluorophore, respectively resulting in to the enhancement of quenching efficiency (Fig. 5b,c). A Similar explanation could be attributed for the effect of fluorophore dose on increase in quenching efficiency [41]. To study the impact of fluorophore dose upon the quenching efficiency, the amount of nanocomposite was varied from 50 mg to 200 mg and the results are presented in Fig. 5d. Quenching efficiency was found to increase with the increase in nanocomposite dose and a maximum of 99.02% fluorescence quenching was observed with 200 mg nanocomposite dose. The enhancement of fluorescence quenching with the increase in fluorophore dose was again due to the probability of increased interactions between the quencher and the analyte. Fig. 5e. depicts the impact of pH on the fluoresce intensity. Maximum quenching was observed in the basic medium due to the dissociation of picric acid and formation of di-anion.

The Stern-Volmer equation for the quenching phenomenon can be represented by Eq. (7) [42].
(7)
F
F O
= 1 +
K SV
Q
where, F, Fo, [Q] and KSV represents final fluorescence intensity, initial fluorescence intensity, solution concentration and Stern-Volmer constant, respectively.

The value of Stern-Volmer constant can be calculated from the plot of
F
F O
versus [Q]. Non-linear Stern-Volmer plot described a dynamic quenching mechanism along with static quenching (Fig. 5f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of various dye sorption parameters for the semi-IPN
ce:section.ce:para: The contact time for the adsorption of biebrich scarlet dye onto semi-IPN (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm) was studied for 8 h at initial dye concentrations of 50 mg L−1. Dye removal percentage for the adsorption of dye molecules was initially found to increase with the increase in contact time, but, after optimum contact time, no significant increase in the dye removal percentage was observed. This was due to the fact that after an optimum contact time of 6 h, saturation was achieved and all the active sites got completely occupied with the adsorbent molecules resulting in no appreciable increase in dye removal percentage (Fig. 6a) [43].

Fig. 6b showed the impact of initial dye concentration on the dye removal efficiency of semi-IPN (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm). It was found that the dye removal percentage achieved a maximum value of 61.48% with 50 mg L−1 initial dye concentration at an optimized time of 6 h in a neutral dye solution. Beyond the optimized dye concentration, no appreciable increase in the dye removal percentage was observed. At lower dye concentration, a fewer number of dye molecules were present in the neighborhood of adsorbent surface having a lower magnitude of driving force to interact with the semi-IPN matrix (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm). With the increase in dye concentration, the extent of driving force increases, resulting in increased dye removal percentage. When all the vacant sites got completely occupied, then there is no appreciable increase in dye removal percentage [44].

The extent of maximum dye removal percentage was calculated through the variation of adsorbent dose from 100 mg to 250 mg in neutral pH under pre-optimized initial dye concentration and contact time. The maximum dye removal percentage (61.70%) was observed for 150 mg adsorbent dose in 30 mL dye solution. With the increase in adsorbent dose, the dye removal percentage was found to increase due to the increase in available active sites. But, after an optimum adsorbent dose, maximum number of dye molecules has sufficient sites for their adsorption and further increase in adsorbent dose don't have an appreciable impact on the dye removal percentage (Fig. 6c) [45].

pH dependent swelling behavior of the semi-IPN (Na-Alg/Chit-cl-polyAAm) was studied in variable pH range (3.0–11.0). Anionic dye biebrich scarlet possessed maximum dye removal potential (92%) in acidic medium (pH =3.0). An increase in pH was resulted into decreased dye adsorption capacity probably due to competitive inhibition of the OH– ions with the negatively charged dye molecules towards the positive centers situated on the semi-IPN matrix (Na-Alg/Chit-cl-polyAAm). No such competitive inhibition was possible in acidic medium and hence maximum dye removal percentage was observed at pH 3.0 (Fig. 6d) [46].

Non-linear regression coefficient values (R2) were calculated with the help of different kinetic models (Langmuir, Freundlich, Temkin and Flory-Huggins) in order to see the applicability of these models to the present study. These models also depict the nature of interaction between dye molecules and adsorbent surface.

Fig. 6e–h presents different adsorption isotherm models for the removal of biebrich scarlet using semi-IPN (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm). The calculated values of R2 were found to be 0.3098, 0.7946, 0.7852 and 0.1296 for the Langmuir, Freundlich, Temkin and Flory Huggin's adsorption isotherm, respectively. R2 = 0.7946 and 0.7852 are more closer to unity and suggested that the adsorption process is favorable through Freundlich and Temkin adsorption isotherms (Table 1 ).

Fig. 6i–l shows the applicability of different kinetic models for the removal of biebrich scarlet using Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm semi-IPN matrix. R2 = 0.995 at 50 mgL−1 of optimized dye concentration is much closer to unity indicating the suitability of 2nd order kinetics for adsorption process (Table 2 ) [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dye removal efficiency of the nanocomposite (Na-Ala/chit-cl-polyAAm/CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The percentage dye removal efficiency of the nanocomposite (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQDs) was studied under the optimized conditions of semi-IPN (i.e. contact time = 6 h, initial dye concentration = 50 mgL−1, adsorbent dose = 150 mg and pH = 3.0). The highest percentage dye removal efficiency of the nanocomposite (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQDs) was found to be 96%. Compared to semi-IPN (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm), the highest dye removal potential was probably due to the availability of an increased number of binding sites after the incursion of CQDs inside the crosslinked matrix of semi-IPN (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm).

Standard procedure was followed for the determination of regeneration and reusability potential of the samples using 1× 10−1 molL−1 NaOH solution [50]. After desorption of dye molecules, the semi-IPN (Na-Alg/Chit-cl-polyAAm) and nanocomposite (Na-Alg/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQD) were reused for the sorption of biebrich scarlet dye and the adsorption was analyzed through UV–visible spectrophotometer. The samples were tested up to first eight consecutive adsorption-desorption cycles and the efficiency was found to be more up to 6 cycles. Afterward, the dye removal efficiency gradually decreases to 29% and 38% for semi-IPN (Na-Alg/Chit-cl-polyAAm) and nanocomposite (Na-Alg/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQD) due to degradation of the superabsorbent matrices and inaccessibility of the active sites (Fig. 7 ). The recyclability experiments suggested the importance of synthesized samples towards industrial applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative study with the already existing adsorbents
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorbents in the presented research work were compared with the already existing adsorbents for the removal of anionic dyes and they were found to be superior to many other adsorbents (Table 3 ).

Sodium alginate and chitosan hybrid backbone displayed a unique choice for the synthesis of semi-IPN (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm) and carbon quantum dots nanocomposite (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQDs). Under optimized reaction parameters, the semi-IPN possessed 600% maximum swelling percentage. The nanocomposite exhibited 99% quenching efficiency for the selective detection of picric acid from a variety of organic solvents. Semi-IPN (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm) and the nanocomposite (Na-Ala/Chit-cl-polyAAm/CQDs) could adsorb 92% and 96% of biebrich scarlet dye at pH 3.0, respectively. The adsorption process was found to be favorable through Freundlich and Temkin adsorption isotherms along with the applicability of 2nd order kinetics. The synthesized samples were found too much better than the other adsorbents due to their recyclability and reusability potential.
